name: Editorial Serif
description: A reading-first style modelled on print magazines and long-form journalism: expressive serif headlines, measured body columns, deliberate vertical rhythm, and restrained colour that lets typography and imagery carry the voice.
visual_principles: Serif or contrast display type for headlines paired with a readable body face
Optimal measure (roughly 60-75 characters) for sustained reading
Strong vertical rhythm and baseline alignment
Generous margins and pull-quotes for pacing
Photography and illustration treated with editorial restraint
accessibility_risks: High-contrast display serifs at small sizes can reduce legibility for low-vision users
Tight tracking and decorative ligatures may impair dyslexic readers
Justified text with poor hyphenation creates rivers and uneven spacing
performance_risks: Multiple custom serif weights add font payload; subset and preload to mitigate
appropriate_contexts: Content where reading comfort and tone of voice are the product
Brands wanting to signal heritage, craft, or intellectual authority
Marketing that competes on substance rather than flash
inappropriate_contexts: Dense application UI, dashboards, and data tables
Highly interactive transactional flows where serifs feel decorative
Tiny-text UI chrome where serif detail muddies at small sizes
evidence: The New York Times, The Atlantic, and Medium demonstrate serif editorial systems optimised for reading
Legibility research (Larson & others) supports measure and rhythm guidance for long-form comprehension
